---
title: Quarto Integration Test
author: Test
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
execute:
  cache: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

Here is something $\zeta^{(s)}$.^[With a footnote.]

Okay.

## Prose and Math {#sec-math}

Here is something $\zeta^{(s)}$.

The normal distribution has probability density function of variable $x$:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$ {#eq-normal}

As shown in @eq-normal, the distribution is parameterized by mean $\mu$
and standard deviation $\sigma$.

## Executable Code

```{python}
import numpy as np

mu, sigma = 0, 1
x = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200)
y = (1 / (sigma * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))) * np.exp(-0.5 * ((x - mu) / sigma) ** 2)
print(f"Peak density: {y.max():.4f}")
```

### Plot Output

```{python}
#| label: fig-density
#| fig-cap: "Standard normal density curve"

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_xlabel('$x$')
ax.set_ylabel('$f(x)$')
ax.set_title('Standard Normal Distribution')
plt.show()
```

As shown in @fig-density, the density is symmetric about zero.

## Callout

You can also use callouts:

::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
This is a Quarto callout block. It should render with special styling
in Quarto but appears as a plain blockquote in Pandoc.
:::

## Cross-references and citations

### Cross-References

See @eq-normal for the density formula and @fig-density for the plot.
The math details are in @sec-math.

### Citation

The normal distribution was studied extensively by Gauss [@gauss1809].
